title: Psalms 111
subtitle: Great Are the Works of the LORD

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “Glorious and Majestic Are His Deeds”

heb.paragraph: "Praise the LORD (halləlū-yāh). I will extol the LORD with all my heart" (v.1). An acrostic psalm (each line begins with a successive Hebrew letter). The works of the LORD are great (v.2), glorious and majestic (v.3), gracious and compassionate (v.4). He provides food for those who fear him; he remembers his covenant forever (v.5).

notes.note: "Hallelujah! I will extol the LORD with all my heart. Great are the works of the LORD, studied by all who delight in them." Worship begins with hallelujah; study follows. Delight and investigation go together.

"He has caused his wonders to be remembered; the LORD is gracious and compassionate. He provides food for those who fear him." Memory, grace, provision — the trio of God’s character toward his people.

refs.note: Great and marvelous are your deeds echoes the psalm's celebration of divine works.
Rehearsing God's mighty acts to demonstrate his uniqueness parallels the psalm's approach.
The incomparably great power demonstrated in Christ continues the psalm's theme.

hist.context: Psalm 111 is an acrostic psalm — each line begins with successive letters of the Hebrew alphabet. It celebrates God's works in creation and redemption. The 'assembly of the upright' (v.1) is the worshiping community where these deeds are remembered. The psalm pairs with Psalm 112, which celebrates the righteous person.

sections.header: Verses 6–10 — “The Fear of the LORD Is the Beginning of Wisdom”

heb.paragraph: "The fear of the LORD is the beginning (rēʾashîṭ) of wisdom; all who follow his precepts have good understanding. To him belongs eternal praise" (v.10). The wisdom tradition’s motto (Prov 1:7; Job 28:28) here closes the psalm. His works are faithful and just (v.7), his precepts trustworthy (v.7), his redemption enduring (v.9). The acrostic’s final line: eternal praise.

notes.note: "The works of his hands are faithful and just; all his precepts are trustworthy. He provided redemption for his people; he ordained his covenant forever." Works + precepts + redemption + covenant: the four pillars of God’s relationship with Israel.
"The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom." The Psalter quotes the wisdom tradition’s foundational verse. Proverbs opens with it (1:7); Job closes with it (28:28); Psalm 111 confirms it.

refs.note: The fear of the LORD as the beginning of wisdom directly echoes the psalm's conclusion.
Fear of the LORD as wisdom's beginning and knowledge of the Holy One parallels the psalm.
Wisdom from above that is pure and peace-loving develops the psalm's wisdom theology.

hist.context: The closing declaration 'the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom' (v.10) links worship to ethics, praise to practical living. Those who follow God's precepts have 'good understanding' — wisdom is demonstrated in obedient life. The acrostic structure aids memorization, making this a teaching psalm.

I will give thanks to the LORD with my whole heart, in the company of the upright, in the congregation.NIVGreat are the works of the Lord; they are pondered by all who delight in them.ESVGreat are the works of the LORD, studied by all who delight in them.

rec.text: ""Hallelujah! I will extol the LORD with all my heart. Great are the works of the LORD, studied by all who delight in them." Worship begins with hallelujah; study follows. Delight and investigation go …"

rows.label: Great Works — 111:1–5
rows.text: Hallelujah. Acrostic. "Great are the works of the LORD." Grace, compassion, food, covenant.

hebtext.note: "Praise the LORD (halləlū-yāh). I will extol the LORD with all my heart" (v.1). An acrostic psalm (each line begins with a successive Hebrew letter). The works of the LORD are great (v.2), glorious and majestic (v.3), gracious and compassionate (v.4). He provides food for those who fear him; he remembers his covenant forever (v.5).

"The fear of the LORD is the beginning (rēʾashîṭ) of wisdom; all who follow his precepts have good understanding. To him belongs eternal praise" (v.10). The wisdom tradition’s motto (Prov 1:7; Job 28:28) here closes the psalm. His works are faithful and just (v.7), his precepts trustworthy (v.7), his redemption enduring (v.9). The acrostic’s final line: eternal praise.

themes.note: Worship peaks: hallelujah acrostic. Holiness: the fear of the LORD as wisdom’s foundation. Covenant: remembered forever.
